data.title: Hexagon: Lower Price Target After Q1'25, But Still A Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I'll be providing an update on Swedish industrial/specialist company Hexagon (OTCPK:HXGBY). While this is a company I've often lauded for its quality of operations and fundamental upside, a quick look at my rating history and overall investment success in the last 1-2 years will tell you two things.

First, you will see that my investment in the company thus far has really gone nowhere, at least from my first positive article, even if I bought some shares at the "lower" price, which you can see below.

Secondly, while I may not be technically up in terms of article RoR, I think it's fairly clear based on the 1-year trend that my recommendations/ratings for the company in terms of "BUY" and "HOLD" have held a certain truth, in the sense that I've been able to identify expensive and appealing price points in that time frame.

Since that particular article in February, we now have the latest quarterly report as well as a drop in share price that has brought the company to a price slightly below my latest "BUY"-rated article.

The question becomes, is the company still a "BUY" here, and what has happened to cause it to drop as much as that?

Let's see what we have here, and what trends 2025- 2026E seem about to bring.

As a reminder, in my last article on the company, my rating for Hexagon was a "HOLD" with a conservative price target (PT) of 110 SEK/share for the native ticker.

Hexagon - Upside possible after the Q1'25 period.

Q1 for 2025, at the time of writing the article, is less than 3 days old and "fresh off the printer", so to speak. The company had both good and less-than-ideal circumstances and trends to report, some of the negatives surprising, some not so.

The fact that geopolitical uncertainty is having a significant global impact should be a surprise to absolutely no one here. It's been something that's been going on for months at this point, and it seems to be that no sector has been spared impact or perceived impact (positive or negative - impact can go one of two ways). In Hexagon's case, the impact is mostly negative. March 2025 saw a meaningful decline in order volumes, and this was expressed specifically in the segments of sensors & robotics solutions, which saw a slowdown, next to the company's license agreements.

However, this failed to stop or slow down the general company trends, where recurring revenues saw an upside of 10%.

This came alongside good margin recovery (gross margin side, operating margins remain impacted by FX, and some volume decline), as well as some demand stabilization in some key sectors, even with the company's end-market remaining uncertain due to the aforementioned instability.

Hexagon is preparing to carve out and split up. The company's plan to separate the Asset Lifecycle Intelligence and the Safety, Infra, and Geospatial divisions is ongoing.

While the robotics solution segment has seen challenges, the company's lineup for the segment for the coming fiscal year of 2025-2026 is strong, and I believe the tariff situation should be viewed as no more than the proverbial "bump in the road" for Hexagon.

Why?

Because despite everything, we still have adjusted gross margins of above 65-66%, we have an EBIT1 margin of above 25%, and the company's cash conversion rates remain beyond competitive. In short, everything that makes Hexagon a good and premium-valued company is still very much there (Source).

Tariff impact is likely to be some of the driving factors behind the coming valuation trends, though, and the reasoning for it is quite easy to see when you look at the volume evolution over the past few months.

What's at risk here? Well, at the current level, the company is seeing non-trivial potential impacts in China, though less than expected, at around 150M SEK or $15M in total tariff impact. The reason for this is that the direct flows between China and the US for Hexagon are already, pre-tariff, almost non-existent. What flows exist to Canada and Mexico indirectly are being redirected through other logistical solutions, and the company is changing the sourcing for key input materials, such as granite, from other sources. The company is also evaluating price increases to offset tariff impacts, but at the same time, as most companies seem to be doing here, it is also evaluating moving some manufacturing to the US to avoid tariffs. However, like any company, I expect Hexagon to weigh into their decision-making process the likelihood of continued tariffs beyond 3-4 years from now.

As of right now, pre-split/spin-off, the company is still a 5-segment business. For the period, only MI and Geosystems really had negative organic growth, with the company's other sectors seeing either stable or decent growth. Operating margins were rocky, though, with a 290 bps decline company-wide, which, of course, goes some way to explain the decline that Hexagon is seeing in valuation.

While gross margins saw no significant decline and continued along its impressive growth trajectory, we have seen for over 10 years, the company needs to "fix" the other issues that are currently sending profits into flat/negative direction for the short term.

The reason for this is simple - since the tail end of 2012, Hexagon has been a company that has hinged on a "premium" valuation. That has been in question, and has been volatile now for over 4 years. I have had the company in my view for about that long as well, wanting to get in when the company's valuation is appealing. However, I did not expect the EBIT to deteriorate quite as much as it has, which is why I'm moderating my expectations for the company even further, despite maintaining my overall price target range and rating for this valuation (meaning that I consider the company a "BUY").

The volatility the company has been through in the last few years since COVID-19 can best be expressed in visual terms.

As you can see, Hexagon has, multiple times, tried to "get back" to a higher premium of around 25-27x P/E, only to unceremoniously crash back down to below 20x P/E. The perception, based on the earnings results and earnings trends, which since 2022 is actually flat (and not expected to improve in 2025E), is that the background is that earnings growth is likely to be insufficient. Furthermore, at less than 1.7% yield even after a crash to below 95 SEK/share, the company seems quite unappealing here from a return perspective as well, when you can get a yield of well above 2% even from a below-average sort of savings account.

That being said, there are a few things to mention here that are in the positive "column" for the company, even with the challenging times and somewhat pressured results here.

First off, Q2 and Q4 tend to be the company's strongest quarters by far. Q1 is typically weak. There's a seasonal pattern to the results, which is typically a cause for downward pressure, as seems to be the case here.

Secondly, we should not forget where the company operates and the breadth of absolute technical expertise that it offers, ranging from movie technical expertise...

...to things like helping Aerospace giants build the next generation commercial aircraft to meet net-zero carbon footprints, to the automotive sector, to municipal housing, to actual advanced autonomous robotics solutions. This is the reason I'm generally positive on Hexagon - it's my play in all of these advanced fields, where I believe the company holds a key technical edge.

The main risks impacting the thesis for Hexagon at this time is, instead, as I see it, the market uncertainty and environment the company is in, as well as the result of the company's separation, which is currently slated for 2026E. That's what's impacting my long-term thesis for the company.

Valuation for Hexagon - Still a "BUY", but lowering my expectation slightly

Hexagon's growth estimates have been lowered - not just by me, but by other analysts as well, going from a 15-18% EPS growth rate in 2026-2027 down to a 10-11% EPS growth estimate in those years. Together with the 2025E decline, this now comes to a 3-year average of below 7%, which goes some way to explain the valuation decline. A company growing less than 7-8% might not be worth upwards of 25-27x P/E, but should perhaps instead trade below an average of 20x P/E.

At least, that's one possible explanation. Using the 15-year normalized average, which is an argument that I have had for a long time with Hexagon, we now see an upside of 14-16% per year to a fair value of 125-150 SEK/share, meaning the company doesn't, at the 15-year average, fulfill my expectations any longer in "every scenario". The fair value of 125-150 SEK/share is what I previously had as part of my range. This fair value P/E multiple is around 20x.

I need to, at this time, lower my targets a bit. In this case, I am lowering my expectations in terms of premium. Ignoring the 5-10 year P/E average, my argument is that the company does not deserve this at the current rate of growth. I say the fair value is now closer to 130 SEK, or a 20x P/E on a forward basis, which means that you need to buy the company at below 95 SEK/share, down from a PT of about 110 SEK/share.

It does mean that the company is a "Buy" here, being priced at 93 SEK at the time of writing for the native ticker HEXA-B, but it nonetheless represents a significant reduction in estimates and prospects for the company. I believe this is justified based on the lower estimated rate of growth, the uncertainty in terms of the company following the split, and the potential for a lower price when (and if) the negative results, including a 5.5% EPS drop for 2025E, materialize.

This is reflected in the current analyst targets as well. Current street targets for the company range between 88-140 SEK PT, with an average of 103 SEK/share. Despite this 103 SEK average, only 6 out of 20 analysts consider the company. "BUY" here, with most (over 50%) at either "HOLD", "underperform", or "SELL". (Source: Tikr.com Paywalled Link)

The company has peers in the Electronic equipment segment, but none that do really what the company does in a comparable way. Some of these include Cognex (CGNX), Renishaw PLC, and Keyence (Japanese company, symbol 6861), but I would consider Hexagon's mix and appeal fairly unique here and not directly comparable in a peer way.

I therefore base my thesis strictly on historical P/E and data, as well as forecasts and what I would consider fair value for the company in question based on this.

As of Q1'25, this is updated to 95 SEK for a PT, meaning the company is currently a "BUY" with a 15% annualized rate of upside to a fair value of 130 SEK/share.

The ADR for Hexagon is HXGBY, a 1x ADR, meaning it comes to around $9.85/share, which at the time of writing makes it a "Buy" but with a smaller upside than 20 cents for the ADR trading at $9.73/share.

Thesis

  • Hexagon is, according to me, perhaps the most attractive positioning industrial/software company in the world. At the right price, this company becomes a must-have, and one you do not look "back" on until it's excessively overvalued. This company is extremely well-capitalized, attractively managed, and has expertise in future-proof global industries with a high upside.
  • However, as of early 2025, I consider the company's growth prospects to be somewhat impaired, and I lower my PT to 95 SEK/share from 110 SEK, moderating my fair value upside target to 130 SEK/share.
  • If bought at this price, the company has a double-digit, 15%+ annualized upside, and is attractive enough for an entry here, but I would choose to invest in other companies above this share price.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of 95 SEK/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "BUY" rating as of May 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Reddit: Remaining Bearish On Valuation Even After Pullback
data.description:
Reddit (NYSE:NYSE:RDDT) has pulled back nearly 50% since February highs as investors weigh on a potential growth slowdown and a more general risk-off appetite to stocks. In fact, RDDT has been down over 30% since my last bearish article on the company. Valuation had reached an aggressive point and even though results have been strong, the stock’s bull run was bound to end.

More recently, RDDT reported very strong quarterly results that were ahead of guidance and expectations. While this immediately sent the shares higher, the stock has since traded off, now retreating to pre-earnings levels.

Reddit remains one of the leading social media platforms with an expanding online community where users can discuss, share, and vote on user-generated content.

My bearish thesis is more driven by the company’s ongoing premium valuation facing a more challenging macro environment. Yes, user growth and retention metrics have remained healthy, however, higher-valuation names with exposure to marketing/advertising spend may be more at risk during turbulent macro periods.

For now, I believe investors should avoid this name until either valuation pulls back to more reasonable levels, or there is greater clarity in the macro environment.

Strong Financial Update

During the company’s most recent quarter, RDDT reported revenue of $392.4 million, growing 61% yoy and beating consensus expectations by over $22 million, a significant revenue beat. On top of that, adjusted EBITDA came in at $115.3 million, representing a margin of 29.4%, significantly higher than the 4.1% in the year-ago period.

Advertising revenue still represents the vast majority of the mix. This revenue segment came in at $358.6 million, growing 61% yoy and representing ~91% of total revenue. Yes, this growth rate is extremely impressive and supports a strong valuation multiple. However, my biggest concern and driver of my bearish thesis revolves around the uncertain macro environment causing companies to pullback on their marketing and advertising spend. Given this represents the vast majority of RDDT’s revenue, a more cautious investment environment would negatively impact RDDT’s growth rate.

To date, this has not been the case. However, fears around higher tariffs, potential trade wars, and a bearish scenario of entering into a recession have been on the rise. Given the ease and flexibility of pulling back advertising spend, this is often one of the early expense items to be cut during a slower growth period.

The biggest drivers behind RDDT’s growth are from daily active uniques (“DAUq”) and average revenue per user.

Overall, DAUq came in at 108.1 million for the quarter, growing 31% yoy. Average revenue per user was very strong at $3.63, growing 23% yoy. However, I believe it is very important to look at these metrics on a geographic level.

With U.S. representing 80% of the company’s revenue, this remains a very important geography. RDDT has done a great job expanding into other countries, calling out success in Brazil this most recent quarter. However, U.S. DAUq grew 21% yoy this past quarter, with U.S. ARPU growing 31% yoy. Thus, the higher revenue per customer drove the majority of U.S. growth this quarter. Although not a bad thing, with ARPU already at a record $6.27, it is a little challenging to envisioning this metric continuing to grow at the current pace.

Although the U.S. market has not yet reached saturation, growth is starting to slowdown. RDDT will need to drive more focus on U.S. ARPU, which may come in the form of increased investments and product updates.

On the international front, DAUq grew a very impressive 41% yoy. However, ARPU only grew 22% yoy to $1.34. While both of these growth metrics are very impressive, in order for RDDT to keep trading at a premium valuation, international growth will need to remain strong, even as the global macro environment potentially deteriorates.

Ongoing success internationally is not given, however, given RDDT’s traction in the U.S. and strong growth across several international regions, RDDT seems to be in good position. The biggest question internationally with revolve around how expensive it will be to gain even more traction and at what pace international ARPU can grow.

In a situation where RDDT would need to accelerate its investments, the company has done a good job in recent quarters to demonstrate its profitability potential. Adjusted EBITDA margins have rapidly expanded from 4.1% last year to 29.4% in the most recent quarter.

Incremental adjusted EBITDA margin has trended above 70% for the 5th consecutive quarter. Although I am a little less confident in RDDT’s ability to maintain such a high revenue growth rate, the company has positively surprised me when it comes to margin expansion. More in this below on valuation, but the higher adjusted EBITDA margin may help support the company’s stock if revenue growth starts to slow down.

For Q2, RDDT guided to revenue of $410-430 million, representing 46-53% yoy growth. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be $110-130 million, or roughly ~28.5% margin at the midpoint. Although revenue growth is expected to decelerate from the 61% yoy growth this past quarter, adjusted EBITDA margin is expected to remain strong.

Valuation

Now here comes the tricky part.

RDDT’s valuation has been on a bit of a rollercoaster over the past year. The stock still predominately trades on a revenue multiple, however as hinted above, the improving adjusted EBITDA may eventually lead to more investors looking at an adjusted EBITDA multiple for valuation.

With RDDT still in “growth mode” as its top priority, it seems as if revenue growth will remain the north star. If RDDT is able to keep beating revenue growth expectations, then it is possible the stock maintains a premium valuation. However, any signs of a potential slowdown, lower marketing/advertising spend across the economy, or less international traction, then I believe the stock could be in a bit of trouble.

International monetization has proven to be more difficult for many social media and streaming companies. Can RDDT still improve the international ARPU. Of course. Will the pace of improvement appease potential concerns around international opportunity? That is the million-dollar question.

The stock currently has a market cap ~$21 billion and with ~$2 billion in net cash (no debt), this results in an enterprise value of ~$19 billion.

Per Yahoo Finance, 2025 revenue is expected to grow 42% yoy to $1.85 billion and 2026 revenue is expected to grow another 28% yoy to $2.37 billion. Although RDDT has proven to pretty consistently beat its growth expectations, the more challenging macro backdrop may challenge this.

Using the above estimates from Yahoo Finance, RDDT currently trades ~8x 2026 revenue. Is this an overly aggressive multiple given RDDT’s financial profile? Absolutely not. But if growth does not continue to beat expectations, then this is where increased risk comes into play.

Looking at the above valuation charts, RDDT continues to trade at a very nice premium relative to other social media companies. Yes, RDDT’s growth profile is much different, however, the current stock price already reflects this bullish optimism. If RDDT were to miss a quarter, guide below expectations, or even beat estimates by not by a large enough amount, the stock could re-rate lower.

Overall, my bearish thesis on RDDT largely hinges on growth expectations being high and the stock continuing to trade at a nice premium. As long as RDDT keeps beating and raising, the stock should be okay. But any signs of cracks in the model, and watch out below.

Risks

The biggest risk to my bearish thesis is that RDDT continues to beat expectations. As seen in recent quarters, the company’s growth profile has been tremendous. More impressively, the adjusted EBITDA margin has significantly improved from last year. I am not arguing that RDDT’s financial profile is bad, in fact, quite the opposite. RDDT has proven it can operate at a high-level.

An additional risk is if advertising spend remains strong. Although there are no signs of deterioration, a slower growth economic environment often leads to less advertising spend. This would negatively impact RDDT as well as the other social media companies. A more resilient advertising spend environment would debunk my bearish thesis.

data.title: Fidelity Freedom 2040 Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Approach

Fidelity Freedom® Funds (the Funds) are designed so that the target date referenced in the Fund name is the approximate year when investors expect to retire. Except for Fidelity Freedom® Income Fund, each of the Funds seeks high total return until reaching its respective target retirement date; thereafter, each Fund's objective will be to seek high current income and, as a secondary objective, capital appreciation. Except for Fidelity Freedom® Income Fund, each Fund's asset allocation strategy becomes increasingly diversified as it approaches its target date – and beyond. Ultimately, the Funds are expected to merge with Fidelity Freedom Income Fund. The Funds employ a disciplined and time-tested investment process focused on helping investors achieve successful retirement outcomes by leveraging the depth and strength of Fidelity's investment research and resources.

1 Life of Fund (LOF) if performance is less than 10 years. Fund inception date: 09/06/2000.

2 This expense ratio is from the most recent prospectus and generally is based on amounts incurred during the most recent fiscal year, or estimated amounts for the current fiscal year in the case of a newly launched fund. It does not include any fee waivers or reimbursements, which would be reflected in the fund's net expense ratio.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate; therefore, you may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance stated. Performance shown is that of the fund's Retail Class shares (if multiclass). You may own another share class of the fund with a different expense structure and, thus, have different returns. To learn more or to obtain the most recent month-end or other share-class performance, visit fidelity.com/performance, institutional.fidelity.com, or 401k.com. Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s): Andrew Dierdorf, Brett Sumsion

Trading Symbol: MUTF:FFFFX

Start Date: September 06, 2000

Size (in millions): $27,520.02

Morningstar Category: Fund Target-Date 2040

Investment performance of the Fidelity Freedom Fund products depends on the performance of the underlying investment options and on the proportion of the assets invested in each underlying investment option. The investment risk of each Fidelity Freedom Fund changes over time as its asset allocation changes. These risks are subject to the asset allocation decisions of the Investment Adviser. Pursuant to the Adviser's ability to use an active asset allocation strategy, investors may be subject to a different risk profile compared to the fund's neutral asset allocation strategy shown in its glide path. The funds are subject to the volatility of the financial markets, including that of equity and fixed income investments in the U.S. and abroad, and may be subject to risks associated with investing in high-yield, small-cap, commodity-linked and foreign securities. Leverage can increase market exposure, magnify investment risks, and cause losses to be realized more quickly. No target date fund is considered a complete retirement program and there is no guarantee any single fund will provide sufficient retirement income at or through retirement. Principal invested is not guaranteed at any time, including at or after the funds' target dates.

Not FDIC Insured • May Lose Value • No Bank Guarantee

Market Update and Portfolio Review

Uncertainty about the direction of U.S. policy weighed on markets in Q1, as investors digested news on tariffs, cuts to government programs, and tighter immigration policy. U.S. equities fared poorly, especially large-cap growth and smaller- caps. Tariffs were a catalyst for the repricing of non-U.S equities. Non-U.S. markets' fiscally oriented growth narrative, cheaper valuations, and improved earnings estimates drove one of the largest relative-return differences between U.S. and non-U.S. equities in history. A falling U.S. dollar also boosted non-U.S. stocks. Fixed-income assets served as an equity diversifier amid near-term risks related to economic growth, inflation and the path of interest rates.

Total Return of Strategic Asset Classes Period Ending March 31, 2025

You cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

U.S. Equities - Dow Jones U.S. Total Stock Market Index, Non-U.S. Equities - MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (Net MA), U.S. Short-Term Inflation- Protected Bond - Bloomberg U.S. TIPS 0-5 Years Index, U.S. Long-Term Inflation- Protected Bond - Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation Notes: 5+ Years Index, U.S. Investment- Grade Bond - Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, Long-Term U.S. Treasury Bond - Bloomberg U.S. Long Treasury Index, International Bond - Bloomberg Global Agg Treasury ex USD, ex EM, RIC Capped, Float Adjusted USD H Index , Short-Term Debt - Bloomberg U.S. 3-6 Month Treasury Bill Index

Fidelity's target date strategies produced positive absolute returns during Q1. Performance for the strategies as of March 31, 2025, is displayed below (net of fees)*.

Quarterly Performance for Representative Funds Period Ending March 31, 2025

See the Performance Summary table in this review for performance reporting on all vintages. Funds shown in this chart are representative samples of where a target-date investor might be at various stages of their life.

Performance Attribution Summary

Active asset allocation decisions and the performance of the underlying portfolio managers contributed to the fund's performance versus the composite index in Q1. In particular, an overweight in non-U.S. equities and underweight in U.S. equities added meaningful value to the fund's relative result. Among the underlying portfolios, non-U.S. equity portfolio managers, in aggregate, notably contributed to the fund's relative performance, as did U.S. investment-grade bond managers.

Relative Contributors (representative across vintages):

U.S. and non-U.S. equity funds with a value-oriented investment philosophy contributed to the fund's relative result this quarter. The largest fund-level individual contributors:

  • Fidelity® Series Large Cap Stock Fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P 500® index. The fund emphasizes companies for which long-term normalized earnings and free-cash-flow are underappreciated by investors. Sector allocations notably contributed to the fund's relative outcome, particularly an underweight in information technology. Overweight positions in GE Aerospace (GE) and Exxon Mobil (XOM) were among the fund's top individual relative contributors. Not owning benchmark stock Tesla (TSLA) also helped.
  • Fidelity® Series International Value Fund outpaced its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Value Index. The fund is managed by Alex Zavratsky, who believes that investing in attractively valued, high-quality companies (e. g., a strong balance sheet, stable return on equity and recurring cash flow) provides the best chance of outperformance in the long run. Outperformance was driven by security selection, primarily in the industrials sector, where an out-of-benchmark position in German auto and arms manufacturer Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) was the largest individual relative contributor. A non-benchmark stake in British-based defense and aerospace firm BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF) also helped, along with an overweight in Banco Santander.
  • Fidelity® Series Emerging Market Opportunities Fund outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. The fund is managed by a team of sector-based portfolio managers who specialize in security selection within their respective sectors. Security selection within the communication services, materials and health care sectors lifted the fund's relative result. Top individual relative contributors included an out-of-benchmark position in Singapore-based digital entertainment, e-commerce and digital finance conglomerate Sea (SE), and overweight stakes in South African firm Impala Platinum Holdings (OTCQX:IMPUY) and multinational commerce group PDD Holdings (PDD).

Relative Detractors (representative across vintages):

U.S. and non-U.S. equity portfolios with a growth bias generally detracted this quarter. The largest fund-level individual detractors:

  • Fidelity® Series Growth Company Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Steve Wymer, who invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue chip growth to aggressive growth. Steve focuses on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches, and those he considers capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Security selection within the health care sector notably detracted from relative performance for the quarter. The largest individual relative detractors included a larger-than-index stake in footwear designer and distributor Deckers Outdoor (DECK), along with semiconductor companies Nvidia (NVDA) and Astera Labs (ALAB).
  • Fidelity® Series Blue Chip Growth Fund trailed its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Sonu Kalra, who invests in companies that he believes have above-average earnings-growth potential and a sustainable business model. Security selection within information technology, including overweights in Marvell Technology (MRVL), Astera Labs and Nvidia, meaningfully detracted from performance. An underweight and security selection in the financials sector also hurt.

Active Asset Allocation:

Active asset allocation positioning had a positive influence on the fund's relative performance this quarter, especially an overweight in non-U.S. equities and an underweight in U.S. equities.

Outlook and Positioning

The fund's glide path and strategic asset allocation reflect our long-term views and insights on participant needs, diversification and capital markets. Our investment process focuses on selecting strategic asset classes that provide appealing long-term returns, independent sources of return and risk, and favorable portfolio implementation attributes. We maintain the view that trends in the economy and policy conditions may imply a regime change for financial markets. High debt, aging demographics, peak globalization, and geopolitics continue to create uncertainty in the path of inflation, policy and corporate profits. With this in mind, strategic asset allocation decisions continue to emphasize diversification to help us navigate different risks. In recent years, we have amplified portfolio diversification based on a view that participants will experience multiple market environments throughout their lifetime, and that regimes can change abruptly. Over long periods, equities have been a powerful asset for building wealth, while our fixed-income portfolio provides balance during periods of inflationary and deflationary stress. The fund's active allocation positioning reflects intermediate-term views on assets that we believe are mispriced relative to our view of fair value. We expect the U.S. economy to muddle through tariff-induced challenges because of its large, diversified, services- based economy, with resilient companies and consumer-related sectors. We also acknowledge the considerable uncertainty about the outlook for the domestic economy, as investors, consumers and businesses assess the likely duration and impact of policy changes. While non-U.S. companies are facing the risk of weaker demand from U.S. consumers, a number of factors may buffer the impact, including lower starting valuations and earnings expectations, fiscal room for governments to offset the impact of tariffs, and the opportunity to repatriate capital that has flowed into U.S. assets over the past decade. We believe the range of possible outcomes is wide and the landscape is changing rapidly. This has caused volatility in both equity and fixed-income markets. We continue to assess incoming information as it occurs, in collaboration with analysts across Fidelity's multi-asset, equity and fixed-income teams. We continue to evaluate the fund's positioning, while adhering to our diversified, long-term approach. In our view, heightened volatility and the persistence of uncertainty in the capital markets reinforce the importance of a disciplined investment process. Our target-date strategies are managed with a long-term investment horizon and provide investors with a diversified portfolio that seeks to balance their need for total returns with their sensitivity to many risks – longevity, market, inflation and deflation. During the 29 years we have managed target-date funds, this approach has proven to be a prudent and time-tested strategy. Since inception of Fidelity's target-date strategies, we believe we have achieved successful outcomes, as measured by total returns and by investors' tendency to remain invested throughout periods of volatility. We remain focused on researching the key drivers of outcomes for retirement plan participants and retirement investors, including capital markets, diversification and future expectations of participant attributes.

TOTAL RETURN OF STRATEGIC ASSET CLASS

A graphical representation of market performance and the variations in returns among strategic asset classes, as represented by the following indexes:

Dow Jones U.S. Total Stock Market Index is a float-adjusted market-capitalization-weighted index of all equity securities of U.S. headquartered companies with readily available price data.

MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (Net MA Tax) is a market- capitalization-weighted index designed to measure the investable equity market performance for global investors of large and mid- cap stocks in developed and emerging markets, excluding the United States. Index returns are adjusted for tax withholding rates applicable to U.S. based mutual funds organized as Massachusetts business trusts.

Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (OTCPK:TIPS) 0-5 Year Index (Series-L) is a market value-weighted index that

measures the performance of inflation-protected securities issued by the US Treasury that have a remaining average life of less than 5 years.

Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (OTCPK:TIPS) 5+ Year Index (Series-L) is a market value-weighted index that measures the performance of inflation-protected securities issued by the US Treasury that have a remaining average life of 5 or more.

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based, market- value-weighted benchmark that measures the performance of the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. Sectors in the index include Treasuries, government- related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS, and CMBS.

Bloomberg U.S. Long Treasury is a market value-weighted index of investment-grade fixed-rate public obligations of the U.S. Treasury with maturities of ten years or more.

Bloomberg fllobal Aggregate Treasury Ex USD Ex EM RIC Capped Float Adjusted Index is a customized subset of the Global

Aggregate Treasury Index that meets the same diversification guidelines that a fund must pass to qualify as a regulated investment company (RIC). This multi-currency benchmark includes

fixed-rate treasury bonds from developed markets issuers while excluding USD denominated debt. The index also adjusts the par amount outstanding of GBP-, JPY-, and CAD-denominated bonds for Central Bank purchases in each of the respective currencies.

Bloomberg U.S. 3-6 Month Treasury Bills Index is a market- capitalization-weighted index of investment-grade, fixed-rate public obligations of the U.S. Treasury with remaining maturities from 3 up to (but not including) 6 months, excluding zero coupon strips.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (INDEX) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

There are multiple drivers of performance for the Funds. Strategic asset allocation is expected to be the primary source of returns over long-term periods, while incremental excess return can result from active asset allocation and building-block manager decisions. Strategic allocation decisions are based on research and insights related to the factors that influence outcomes over long-term periods, including diversification, capital markets and participant behaviors. We periodically increase the diversification of our strategic asset allocation in an attempt to better navigate capital market uncertainty. Active asset allocation is intended to provide shareholders with exposure to asset classes that are mispriced and may offer additional returns and opportunities for risk mitigation. Active allocation expands the investment opportunity set and provides flexibility to adjust asset class exposures over intermediate-term periods. Allocations to active building-block managers offer shareholders the opportunity to benefit from informational advantages across Fidelity's asset management platform.

RANKING INFORMATION

© 2025 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved. The Morningstar information contained herein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or redistributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Fidelity does not review the Morningstar data and, for mutual fund performance, you should check the fund's current prospectus for the most up-to-date information concerning applicable loads, fees and expenses.

% Rank in Morningstar Category is the fund's total-return percentile rank relative to all funds that have the same Morningstar Category. The highest (or most favorable) percentile rank is 1 and the lowest (or least favorable) percentile rank is 100. The top- performing fund in a category will always receive a rank of 1%. % Rank in Morningstar Category is based on total returns which include reinvested dividends and capital gains, if any, and exclude sales charges. Multiple share classes of a fund have a common portfolio but impose different expense structures.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Views expressed are through the end of the period stated and do not necessarily represent the views of Fidelity. Views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and Fidelity disclaims any responsibility to update such views. These views may not be relied on as investment advice and, because investment decisions for a Fidelity fund are based on numerous factors, may not be relied on as an indication of trading intent on behalf of any Fidelity fund. The securities mentioned are not necessarily holdings invested in by the portfolio manager(s) or FMR LLC. References to specific company securities should not be construed as recommendations or investment advice.

Diversification does not ensure a profit or guarantee against a loss.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R- Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

Other third-party marks appearing herein are the property of their respective owners.

data.title: Bandwidth Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Bandwidth Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Undervalued Profit Growth
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) obliterated profit and sales estimates for its first quarter on April 30, 2025. The social-media behemoth reported $6.43 per share in profits, on an adjusted basis, which compares to an estimate of $5.28 per share.

First quarter sales surged 16% YoY to $42.31 billion, topping the consensus by a cool $1.21 billion. What was eye-catching in Meta Platforms’ earnings presentation was the massive growth in operating and net profits amid a positive spending environment for large social-media companies.

In my last piece on Meta Platforms, ‘Meta Platforms: Why You'll Regret Not Buying The Stock Today’, I underscored that the social-media platform had potential to grow its operating profits at lightning speed and Meta did not disappoint: The company reported substantially faster growth in its operating profits in 1Q25.

I think that Meta Platforms, taking into account that the stock is now selling for just 21x 2026e profits, should be a core holding for investors that want to build a diversified portfolio in the fastest growing technology companies in Silicon Valley.

Advertising Business On Fire, Operating And Net Profits Are Soaring

Meta Platforms’ first quarter sales climbed to $42.3 billion, up 16% YoY on robust demand for online ads and Meta’s advertisement placement services. Interestingly, Meta Platforms’ operating profits surged at a much higher rate; they went up 27% YoY.

Both ad impressions and ad prices increased 10% for Meta Platforms in 1Q25 which is a reflection of robust demand for Meta ads from marketers.

As a consequence, Meta Platforms’ operating profits climbed to $17.6 billion in 1Q25 whereas the company’s net profits totaled $16.6 billion, up 35% YoY.

Meta Platforms is one of the most profitable software companies in the world and in the first quarter the platform had an operating profit margin to 41%, down 7 percentage points QoQ.

Meta Platforms also produced strong net income profitability with margins of 39% in the March-quarter. The decrease in operating and net profit margins is due to sales growth slowing down in 1Q25 which is a recurring phenomenon for social-media company that see cutbacks ad spending following the Christmas quarter.

Meta Platforms’ apps still dominate social media user preferences which is poised to bode well for investors that anticipate a sustained operating profit up-scaling in the years ahead.

All of the company’s apps, Instagram, Facebook and WhatsApp, are still very much preferred destinations for social-media users, with all three of them ranked in the top five social-media platforms, based on Meltwater’s Digital 2025 Report.

The advertising reach of Meta Platforms is also still unparalleled with Facebook alone reaching 2.3 billion accounts which obviously makes Facebook a preferred ad spending platform for marketers.

CAPEX Plan Points To Ongoing Growth In Operating Income

Not only did Meta Platforms handily surpass profit and sales estimates for the last quarter, but the company is also enacting an ambitious CAPEX program with management planning to spend up to $72 billion this year at the high-end while the low-end forecast still calls for $64 billion in spending.

The forecast was raised from a prior forecast of $60-65 billion and reflects a 73% YoY jump in Meta’s capital budget. The majority of this CAPEX will go to data centers which are needed to power Meta Platforms’ artificial intelligence efforts.

AI is obviously a key growth area for the social-media company and I think that we could be seeing new initiatives for Meta AI, smart glasses or new augmented reality products.

Pay 21x 2026e Profits For The Largest U.S. Social Media Company, Growing At Double Digits

Despite burgeoning operating profitability, Meta Platforms is selling for a very moderate Price-To-Earnings multiple. Meta Platforms is presently selling for a 2026 estimated profit multiple of 21.1x which benchmarks against a peer group average Price-To-Earnings ratio is 16.6x (see the table below).

Meta Platforms is not the only social-media company in the U.S. and it competes against a number of differentiated platforms that target different audiences such as Etsy Inc. (ETSY), Pinterest Inc. (PINS) or Snap Inc. (SNAP).

Etsy, for instance, is an artist-focused social-media platform that is presently selling for a 16.0 times leading earnings and is expected to produce significant (67%) profit growth next year.

Pinterest, a picture-oriented discovery platform, is selling for a 12.7 times leading profit multiple while Snap, thanks to its estimated loss for next year, cannot be valued with a Price-To-Earnings ratio.

Based on the peer group average growth rate of 30% next year, Meta Platforms is going to grow at a substantially slower pace (11%) than other social-media platforms. That said, Meta Platforms is the only large social-media platform that I would want to own taken into account its reach in accounts as well as profitability and operating income up-scaling opportunity.

Meta Platforms is substantially bigger than all the other social-media platforms combined which should ensure that incremental growth in advertising spend finds its way primarily to Meta Platforms’ services. Hence, with online marketing spending only poised to grow in the future, the biggest beneficiary of this incremental spending in the U.S. should be Meta Platforms.

Since META is the biggest social-media platform available to users and outclasses its smaller peers in terms of popularity and reach, it also has the biggest potential to launch new products and services, like AI-powered smart glasses, which could give Meta Platforms’ a lever to up-scale its operating profits much quicker than smaller social-media platforms.

As an investor, I would be willing to pay a ‘size’ or ‘scale’ premium for Meta Platforms because of this significant advantage. As a consequence of Meta Platforms’ substantial operating profits, the company is also in a good position to either acquire other promising apps or social networking sites, should its core business slow down.

Meta Platforms changed hands for 26x 2026 estimated profits in February before investors got rattled by tariffs and the possibility of an economic downturn.

I think that Meta Platforms could possibly sell for 25x leading profits which equates to an implied intrinsic value of $708. That said, Meta Platforms will be reliant on the health of the U.S. economy and a possible recession would probably also affect the propensity of marketers to spend money on online ads.

Why I Might Be Wrong About Meta Platforms

Meta Platforms is clearly competitive when bench-marked against other social-media platforms, particularly YouTube, which belongs to Alphabet Inc. (GOOG),and TikTok, which belongs to China-based Bytedance (see the comparison chart above).

My concern with regards to Meta Platforms primarily relates to the company’s ability to defend itself against potentially new, emergent social networks that make better use of AI or promise a more immersive user experience.

The next popular social-media app could mark a serious challenge to established social-media companies, particularly if new apps prove capable of luring younger audiences away from Meta Platforms’s services.

Moreover, a pullback in marketer spending on online ads might be a headwind for Meta Platforms which has built a one-revenue stream business model and so far not succeeded in broadening its sales base.

My Takeaway

Meta Platforms offers investors the opportunity to harness double digit sales and operating profit growth thanks to the company’s high rankings in social-media platform relevancy: The social-media company’s apps are all top destinations for social media users and therefore have relevance for marketers that run digital advertising campaigns on either Facebook, Instagram or WhatsApp.

My personal view on Meta Platforms is that the company has considerable potential for ongoing operating income up-scaling, which relates back to the company’s large reach and aggressive CAPEX plans.

These plans, which center on developing new products with AI, should be expected to add incremental operating profits to Meta Platforms' business moving forward.

To top things off, I think that Meta Platforms’ 21x leading profit multiple is quite reasonable considering the tens of billions of dollars the social-media powerhouse rakes in on a quarterly basis in net and operating profits.

data.title: Waymo Vs. Zoox Vs. Tesla Vs. Pony - We Think Elon Musk Will Lose
data.description:
Waymo (NASDAQ: GOOGL) reports that it's crossed 250K paid rides per week in the United States. That's a business that's now earning more than $100 million per year. As the "self-driving wars" head up, the purpose of this article is to highlight who we think the winners will be (Google) and the losers (Tesla) (NASDAQ: TSLA).

The List (and Business Importance)

With the end of market leader Cruise, a sign of how technologically complicated this problem is, the list of serious competitors in self-driving is effectively 4 long in our view.

  • Waymo (backed by Google)
  • Tesla
  • Zoox (backed by Amazon AMZN)
  • Pony.AI (PONY)

The companies in the list vary pretty dramatically in where they are. Waymo has a fully operating self-driving business with hundreds of cars in major U.S. cities. Zoox has custom-built self-driving vehicles running driverless testing, as the company has moved past retrofitting vehicles, but it's not actually earning money yet.

Tesla has earned billions from FSD subscriptions sold on its cars and has leading "cruise control" technology but isn't running large-scale FSD trials yet. Pony.AI has made major partners, including with Uber recently, and similarly doesn't have large-scale driving yet, but is also focused on autonomous trucking in China.

Backing up here, one aspect worth highlighting is the importance of success to the company.

Let's take Amazon. Amazon paid $1.2 billion for Zoox, and has invested in it since. However, as a $2 trillion company, if Zoox were to disappear tomorrow, it would make up well below 1% of Amazon's value. Similarly, Waymo's latest funding round put it at a $45 billion valuation, or just over 2% of Google's valuation which is "something" but not needle moving.

On the other extreme you have Pony.AI, a $5 billion publicly traded company after its IPO last year with one goal. In between you have Tesla, who's CEO Elon Musk has said it's key to the company's future success and current valuation, but who's current business is manufacturing and selling cars.

Technological Approaches

There's also a lot of variance in technological approaches. Everyone is using artificial intelligence and some aspect of sensor fusion.

However, Tesla's Elon Musk has said so far that the company will not use Lidar and only use camera technology. There are questions about whether a camera-only approach works (Tesla is facing investor lawsuits for its claims) and exactly how that pans out remains to be seen. However, it's worth noting it's not that Tesla doesn't have access to Lidar, it's just not including it for now.

At the end of the day, we don't see one technological approach as ground-breaking versus the other.

Self-Driving Miles Driven

An indicator of how these companies are performing is how many miles are they actually driving with self-driving and how are they performing with it. The below statistics are from the California DMV, but California is the leading state for self-driving miles.

In terms of miles per disengagement, a statistic to indicate how far they go before a human takes over, there's pretty clear jumps between the leaders and the ones behind. Note that Cruise no longer exists as a competitor, and the same is true for Apple. It's also worth highlighting there is a reason that Tesla isn't here, the company didn't actually test full self-driving in 2024.

Another major piece of these is how many miles are the companies driving. Waymo is the clear leader here, earning revenue, with almost 2.4 million miles driven. For perspective, that's ~0.0001% of all miles that're driven on U.S. roads. Zoox, with its miles per disengagement and almost 1 million miles with its Amazon backing is another clear choice.

Nuro is an underdog in both miles driven and miles per disengagement, with a $6 billion valuation that was a down round from 4-years ago. Pony.AI leads in disengagement but is driving fewer miles. However, we do want to highlight that Waymo and Zoox are both leading the pack, and Tesla isn't testing at all.

Total Addressable Market

In terms of total addressable, the market is huge, with Americans driving more than 3 trillion miles per year on all roads.

Waymo is young, but its pricing ranges from $0.2 / mile on top of its flat flee to $1.2 / mile in San Francisco and Los Angeles. That implies the potential of hundreds of billions to trillions of dollars worth of revenue. Putting an exact profit margin on that is incredibly difficult, however, we do want to highlight that we think it'll be a very competitive business.

Tesla did its own analysis, reportedly suppressed by Elon Musk, that figured with competition it would be hard to ever drive a true profit from the service, and it would be a very volatile rollout. We're not particularly surprised at all with that conclusion.

Specifically, should more than one company hit full self-driving, when you don't have hiring employees etc. in the cost basis, the self-driving becomes a scaling problem. Waymo appears to have recognized this, it's partnered to build a new factory in Arizona with the ability to build thousands of vehicles. The company is scaling, but it's a slow and steady process.

Tesla has the scale for manufacturing that its peers don't have, however, with minimal self-driving projects from the traditional mega manufacturers after Cruise (i.e. Toyota, Honda, etc.) we don't think it'll be hard for Waymo etc. to expand partnerships as demand grows. Zoox builds its own robotaxis but it's backed by Amazon, a company that knows a thing or two about scaling.

Attempting to put a valuation on it though, vehicle costs per mile are ~$0.81. We presume self-driving at scale will need to be less than that. Zoox's operating costs are roughly $0.42 / mile, and we expect companies to move towards self-driving. At 3.4 trillion miles per year, that would imply maximum profits of just over $1 trillion.

Assuming 25% of that to justify switching by drivers and that's still $10s of billions in potential annual profit.

Our View

For those who're looking to invest in the self-driving revolution, we recommend investing in either Waymo or Zoox through Alphabet or Amazon. These are both companies that're at the forefront of self-driving, Waymo is actively driving revenue, and they're scaling up permits / handling the very real process of carrying users.

Waymo has already driven customers for millions of miles with no major accidents, which is better than human drivers. Pony.AI is also doing well, but it's a smaller company without a big financial backer. However, it is a pure-play investment, which enables investors to potentially earn big rewards at the cost of big potential losses.

However, the company we don't recommend investing in this list is Tesla. Specifically for 2 reasons:

1. Poor Market Positioning

As discussed above, Tesla isn't leading in self-driving testing. The company isn't operating full customer-centric self-driving yet and at the same time, competitors like Waymo are ramping up vehicle counts. First-mover advantage is a big deal and that's a real concern.

2. Reward vs. Valuation

We discuss this more in our article in Tesla here, however, the majority of Tesla's valuation hinges with success in self-driving. That means that not only is the upside more limited, but the downside is substantial.

3. Lawsuit Risk

The company has already promised self-driving to numerous users and it's facing lawsuits over claims that it has made. Even Grok, through xAI, knows self-driving was first promised almost a decade ago. That increases the potential risks and downside.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the underdog. Our view is that increasingly it looks like self-driving is a bunch of patched together incremental improvements and solutions that make it better than a human driver and enable it to scale, there's no single magical breakthrough that enables flawless self-driving.

However, should such a break-through emerge, at an underdog, it could upend the market and give a single company a commanding share.

Conclusion

The self-driving wars are scaling up. That's despite numerous dedicated companies having a lower valuation today than they had several years ago. However, Waymo is now earning more than $100 million per year in revenue and scaling up rapidly, the company has recently announced deals for a new factory to scale up.

Despite that, in every new field of discovery, there are differences between opportunity and overvalued. For those looking to invest, we see Google and Amazon as effectively managing the downside risks while retaining upside potential given their opportunities. We see Tesla as an overvalued company in the segment and a poor investment.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.
